story.description: A clip of trees, roots, canopy, bark, or forests: sprawling oak tree in sunlit forest setting
story.hook: Deer can aim each ear toward danger
story.lead: Oaks warn each other through roots. Here's what blew my mind: their roots grow right into each other. That's why, when a deer starts chewing one, the connected trees get chemical signals. I just noticed their bark stays scuffed where the roots meet. You ever see moss on an oak trunk? That’s where the roots are closest. So next time you hug a tree, you might be standing in a warning line.

story.lead: Clouds aren't moving at all — it's the camera panning. That's why they streak like that. Those puffy ones travel 10 to 30 mph with the wind, but you'd never guess from their shape. story.description: A clip of mountains, glaciers, rock layers, erosion, or alpine landscapes: footage of rock formations
story.hook: Watch the rock layers; it explains the behavior
story.lead: These cliffs don't stack straight. See how the layers tilt left while the right side stays flat. That's because the flat side was buried under ice for thousands of years. The ice pressed down like a vise, squeezing the rock sideways. These cliffs aren't new—they're old land bent by ancient glaciers. You ever notice how mountains look like they're leaning. Now you know why.
